title: Repairing a blown external render patch
subtitle: Cut out and rebuild a hollow section of render on an outside wall
excerpt: How to identify and repair a section of external render that has blown loose from the masonry behind it, covering how to tap-test to find the extent of the failure, cut back to sound render, apply a bonding coat and a finish coat, and match the surface texture to the surrounding wall.
sourceNotes: BS EN 998-1: Specification for mortar for masonry (rendering and plastering). BRE Good Repair Guide 38: External render (open-access). City and Guilds Level 2 Plastering. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: A section of external wall render that has lost its bond with the masonry behind, creating a hollow cavity between the render and the wall. Identifiable by a dull, hollow sound when tapped with a knuckle. Blown render is caused by moisture penetration behind the render, frost action expanding the void, or a weak bond at installation. It must be removed and replaced; it cannot be re-glued in place.

The first coat of render applied directly to the masonry. Also called the scratch coat in traditional rendering. Mixed to a stronger ratio than the finish coat (typically 1 part cement to 3 parts sand with a plasticiser) and scratched with a comb scratcher before it sets to give the next coat a mechanical key. The bonding coat is the structural layer; the finish coat is purely the weather-resistant surface.

content.text: External render keeps water out when sound. Once

, water gets in behind the wall where it cannot dry out, and damage spreads to the masonry around it. The repair must cut back to solid render on all sides. Patching over it does not work. The blown area has lost its key to the wall and will fail again quickly.

Sand-and-cement render mix: 3 parts sharp sand to 1 part Portland cement for the bonding coat; 4 parts sand to 1 part cement for the finish coat. A plasticiser additive (improves workability and reduces cracking). SBR bonding agent (styrene-butadiene rubber emulsion) brushed onto the bare masonry before the bonding coat to improve adhesion on smooth or low-suction surfaces. Texture spray or dry-dashing aggregate if the surrounding render has a textured or pebble-dashed finish.

Tap across the render surface with a knuckle, moving in a grid pattern. Sound, well-bonded render gives a dense knock; blown render produces a dull, hollow resonance. Mark the boundary of the hollow area with a pencil. Extend the investigation 200 mm beyond the visible crack or damage: blown areas are almost always larger than the visible surface signs suggest.
Step 2, cut back to sound render

Score the perimeter of the hollow area with the angle grinder fitted with a masonry disc. Score at least 50 mm into the sound render surrounding the blown patch to create a clean, square edge. Do not score in a V; a square-edged patch holds better than a tapered one. Use the SDS drill with a chisel bit to break out all the blown render within the scored perimeter. Continue beyond the scored line only where the knock test confirms the render is also hollow.
Step 3, prepare the masonry and apply the bonding coat
Brush all loose material off the masonry face. Apply SBR bonding agent diluted at 1:1 with water to the bare masonry; allow to become tacky before applying the

. Load the hawk with a thumb-sized charge of the stronger 3:1 mix and apply to the masonry with firm trowel strokes, building the coat to within 10 mm of the surrounding render face. Immediately scratch the surface with a serrated trowel or the point of the trowel in a crosshatch pattern. Allow to cure for at least 24 hours before applying the finish coat.
Step 4, apply the finish coat and match the texture

Mix the finish coat at 4:1 sand to cement. Apply with the trowel, filling the repair to match the level of the surrounding render. Feather the edges where the new render meets the old. Texture immediately to match the surrounding finish: for a smooth render, stroke the plasterer's float across the surface in slow circular sweeps; for a sponge-floated texture, use a damp sponge in circular motions while the render is still green. For a pebble-dash finish, fling the aggregate into the still-wet render by hand while a second person holds the dust sheet; this requires practice and ideally a test area first.

Protect the fresh render from direct sun and drying wind for at least 3 days; mist lightly if conditions are hot. Allow to cure for 28 days before applying masonry paint; painting over uncured render traps shrinkage moisture and leads to paint blistering. The new render will almost certainly be a lighter colour than the surrounding wall until it weathers; painting the whole wall elevation rather than the repair alone gives the best visual result.

attrs.symptom: Repair cracks within a few weeks of completion
attrs.cause: The bonding coat was not adequately keyed before the finish coat went on; or the repair dried too fast without a curing period.
attrs.fix: If the crack is a hairline at the patch edge, fill with exterior flexible filler and repaint. If the crack runs across the centre of the patch, the repair has failed; strip back and repeat with a better-keyed bonding coat and a longer cure between coats.

attrs.symptom: Render slumps or falls away from the wall during application
attrs.cause: Mix too wet, or applied in too thick a single coat.
attrs.fix: Remove the collapsed material. Let the bonding coat fully cure, re-wet the surface, and apply the finish coat in a thinner layer (no more than 10 mm in one pass). Let each layer firm before the next.

attrs.symptom: The repaired area blows again within two or three winters
attrs.cause: The underlying cause of the original failure (blocked guttering, missing DPC, bridged cavity) was not addressed.
attrs.fix: Investigate and correct the water source before making any further render repairs. Render cannot waterproof a wall if water is entering from behind it.
